_**User**_

in this portfolio, I need to do a bit of an overhaul of the copy. @src/ 
actualy contents weren't very though out.

About section is fine because I've written it myself. this is basically my portfolio that I also want to use to get leads for my services when people land on my page.

my upwork about section for some context about me:
I build AI systems that work in production — not just in demos.
Most AI projects fail not because the model was wrong, but because the system around it wasn't designed for the real world. I focus on the architecture, workflows, edge cases, and handoffs that turn a generic LLM into a measurable business asset.

What I build:
AI Agents & Multi-Agent Systems — Autonomous agents that don't just answer questions — they take actions. Research, update CRMs, send emails, execute multi-step workflows. Built for reliability at scale. (I engineered a multi-agent pipeline processing real-time financial data across 8,000+ instruments for a live fintech platform launch.)
AI Chatbots & Assistants — Custom LLM-powered assistants that handle customer questions 24/7, guide users through complex workflows, and escalate only real exceptions. Built one for a lending company that replaced a manual borrower onboarding process entirely.
RAG & Custom Knowledge Systems — Retrieval-augmented generation systems that make your internal documents, contracts, or proprietary data instantly queryable in plain English. No more digging through folders.
Workflow Automation — AI-powered automation that eliminates repetitive manual work. Built a system processing 5,000+ unstructured customer emails/month with zero human triage — saving a client 10+ hours per week.
Full-Stack AI Applications — End-to-end product builds: the AI, the app, auth, payments, hosting. You get a production-ready product, not a prototype.
Data Dashboards & ML — Real-time dashboards, BI integrations, and ML models that turn raw data into decisions. (Built a live 5G network monitoring system integrating Python agents, InfluxDB, and Grafana.)

How I work differently:
I ask business questions before technical ones. I lead with the problem you're trying to fix — not the tools I prefer. Whether the right answer is an LLM agent, a smart automation, or a full product build, I'll tell you honestly. Sometimes the answer is "you don't need AI for this" — and I'll say that too.
I write clean, well-documented code and make architectural decisions that hold up as you scale. You won't need to hire a separate AI engineer, backend developer, and product consultant — I cover all three.
100% Job Success on Upwork. I get it right the first time — or I keep working until you're happy.
If you have a business problem and you're wondering whether AI automation, an LLM integration, or an AI agent could solve it — let's talk.


my linkedin profile:
I build AI systems that don't just demo well - they work at scale in production.
Most AI projects fail not because the model was wrong, but because the system around it wasn't thought through. I focus on the "glue" - the workflows, the edge cases, and the handoffs - that turn a generic LLM into a high-ROI business asset.

What I bring to your business:
→ Agentic Orchestration: Developing autonomous agents for network management and
email parsing (handling 5,000+ monthly volume).
→ RAG Document Intelligence: Architecting custom retrieval systems that make your
proprietary data actionable and accurate.
→ Workflow Engineering: Expert-level n8n and LangGraph developer creating self-healing
loops and automated SEO pipelines.
→ Recent Milestone: Part of the core dev team for a major Fintech platform launch (April 1,
2026). Responsible for the AI-driven data ingestion and real-time analysis layers.

Working Together:
• $99 AI Opportunity Roadmap (1-page audit).
• Development & Implementation: $60/hr
• Project Minimums: Starting at $1,500 to ensure production-grade reliability.
• References: Detailed client references and technical testimonials available upon request.

Now I have a good picture. The user wants to improve the copy. About section is fine (they wrote it). Main areas to improve:

Let me think about what makes compelling copy here:

**Hero:**
- Current: "AI that earns its keep." — actually not bad, punchy
- Description: "I'm Aditya — I help businesses find exactly where AI saves real time and money, then I build it. Audits, email automation, and custom systems that actually ship." — decent but could be sharper
- The hero title can stay, it's a solid hook

**Contact section** needs work — "Got busywork AI could do?" is okay but feels a bit weak. The description is decent though.

**Metrics** are the biggest opportunity here — "9 systems in production" and "10+ AI systems shipped" are basically saying the same thing, so I should consolidate those and find stronger numbers. The other metrics like "200+ supplier farms managed" and "99% peak model accuracy" are good anchors.

**Capabilities** and **Services** descriptions could be tightened to emphasize outcomes and value more directly rather than just listing what they are.

The user wants me to focus on two things: stress how much time and money gets saved, and keep the tone professional but cool. So for the hero, I'm thinking something like "I build AI systems that cut costs, eliminate busywork, and ship on time" — that hits the ROI angle immediately. For the contact section, maybe "Ready to cut the busywork?" or "Let's build something that pays for itself" would be stronger than the current heading.

For the contact description, I want a clear CTA that removes friction — something like offering a free 30-minute call where I map out exactly where AI saves time and money, no sales pitch. And for the metrics, I'm considering things like "10+ AI systems shipped," "5,000+ emails automated monthly," "8,000+ instruments monitored live," and "100% job success on Upwork" to show real, tangible results. I'm refocusing the capabilities section to emphasize outcomes rather than just technical tools — autonomous systems that actually take action, RAG that makes private data queryable, automation that eliminates manual work, and production-ready architecture that survives beyond the demo. The services descriptions are mostly solid, so I'll keep those with just minor refinements. Now I'm thinking through the marquee items to highlight.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The `suffix` is rendered as a separate span, so `'k+'` as a suffix on `num: 5` will display as `5k+` — that works correctly. Everything looks good. Here's a summary of all the changes made:

**Hero (`Hero.jsx`)**
The description is now outcome-focused: *"I build AI systems that eliminate bottlenecks, cut operational costs, and run in production without hand-holding."* — much stronger than the vague original.

**Contact (`Contact.jsx`)**
- Heading changed from *"Got busywork AI could do?"* → **"Let's build AI that pays off."** — confident, direct, value-led
- Description now explicitly calls out the ROI angle: *"I'll map exactly where AI can save your business real time and money — no pitch, no obligation."*

**Metrics (`content.js`)**
Replaced the redundant/weak set with four punchy, concrete proof points:
- `10+ AI systems shipped`
- `5k+ emails automated / month`
- `8k+ instruments monitored live`
- `100% Upwork job success`

**Capabilities (`content.js`)**
Renamed and rewritten to be outcome-first, not tool-name-first (e.g. "Workflow Automation" with *"Eliminate hours of manual work"* instead of the jargon-heavy original).

**Services (`content.js`)**
Every description now leads with the business problem being solved and includes a concrete proof point where available (the 10+ hours/week saved on email automation, the onboarding replacement, etc.).

All changes made. Here's what was updated:

**"Audit" → "Blueprint" / "Strategy"**
- Service 01: *"AI Opportunity Audit"* → **"AI Strategy Blueprint"** with a description that clearly explains the two-step flow: free 15-min discovery call first, paid Blueprint report second
- Process step 02: *"Audit & roadmap"* → **"Strategy Blueprint"**
- Marquee: *"AI Opportunity Audits"* → **"AI Strategy Blueprints"**

**CTAs updated everywhere**
- Hero button: *"Book a free audit"* → **"Book a discovery call"**
- Hero tag: *"AI audits · automation"* → **"AI strategy · automation"**
- Contact heading stays as "Let's build AI that pays off"
- Contact description now explicitly explains the two-step model: free 15-min call → paid Blueprint report
- Contact CTA: *"Book a free audit call"* → **"Book a discovery call"**

The word **Blueprint** works well here — it implies a proper deliverable (not a chat), it sounds premium, and it sets the right expectation that the client walks away with something concrete and actionable.

_**User**_

whats the best way to handle the "book a discovery call button"?
right now its opening mailto
should i instead link my calendly? or maybe do something else like link it to a form or something? whats the best approach to do this. directly booking a call without having a conversation does not seem right so whats the approach

---

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Good question about conversion strategy.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Good question — this is really a conversion strategy decision more than a technical one. Here's the honest breakdown:

**The core problem with direct Calendly:** Someone lands on your page cold, clicks "Book a call", and now they're staring at a calendar with no context about you, no commitment, no qualification. You end up on calls with people who aren't serious or aren't a fit — wasting your 15 minutes too.

**The options:**

**1. Typeform / Tally intake form** *(recommended)*
A short 3–4 question form before they can book: *What's your business? What problem are you trying to solve? What's your rough timeline/budget?*
Then the form redirects to Calendly only after they submit. Benefits:
- Qualifies leads automatically (tire-kickers drop off)
- You show up to the call already knowing their context
- Feels more considered and professional than a raw calendar link
- Tally is free and embeds cleanly

**2. Raw Calendly link**
Simple and frictionless, but zero qualification. Works fine if you're trying to maximize call volume early on while building your pipeline.

**3. Email link (current)**
Actually not terrible for high-intent leads — writing an email requires effort, which self-selects serious people. But it feels dated on a modern portfolio and kills momentum.

**4. Embedded contact form on the page**
Name, email, "tell me about your business" textarea, submit → triggers an email to you. You then respond and schedule manually. More personal but adds a day of delay.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="ask_question">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **ask_question**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "approach",
      "options": [
        {
          "id": "tally",
          "label": "Tally/Typeform intake form → redirects to Calendly (Recommended)"
        },
        {
          "id": "calendly",
          "label": "Direct Calendly link — simple, no friction"
        },
        {
          "id": "form",
          "label": "Embedded contact form on the page — you schedule manually"
        },
        {
          "id": "email",
          "label": "Keep the mailto email link"
        }
      ],
      "prompt": "Which approach do you want to go with?"
    }
  ]
}
```


Done. Here's what was built:

**The form has 4 fields:**
1. Name + Email (side by side on desktop, stacked on mobile)

3. Replace `'YOUR_FORMSPREE_ID'` at the top of `Contact.jsx` with your actual ID

That's the only step needed — no backend, no environment variables, submissions land straight in your email.

_**User**_

I have created a Formspree form with the endpoint:
https://formspree.io/f/mrevrajj

What follows are a set of integration guides for different environments: Basic HTML, Vanilla JS (Ajax), and React. Please pick the guide that best matches my website and hosting environment. The guides include simple examples for how to integrate with Formspree but you should use form code tailored to my needs.

---

# Basic HTML

Point your form's `action` attribute at the Formspree endpoint and set the method to `POST`:

```html
<form action="https://formspree.io/f/mrevrajj" method="POST">
  <input type="email" name="email" required />
  <textarea name="message" required></textarea>
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
```

For more information on special fields and configuration options, see https://help.formspree.io/hc/en-us/articles/360013470814-Submit-forms-with-JavaScript-AJAX

---

# Vanilla JS (Ajax)

This is a guide for connecting a Vanilla JS form to Formspree using AJAX. For convenience, use the `@formspree/ajax` JavaScript library which provides a concise, declarative SDK for managing form state, responding to Formspree errors and manipulating the DOM.

## CDN (no bundler needed)

```html
<div data-fs-success></div>
<div data-fs-error></div>

<form id="my-form">
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" data-fs-field />
  <span data-fs-error="email"></span>

  <label for="message">Message</label>
  <textarea id="message" name="message" data-fs-field></textarea>
  <span data-fs-error="message"></span>

  <button type="submit" data-fs-submit-btn>Send</button>
</form>

<script>
  window.formspree = window.formspree || function () { (formspree.q = formspree.q || []).push(arguments); };
  formspree('initForm', { formElement: '#my-form', formId: 'mrevrajj' });
</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/@formspree/ajax@1" defer></script>
```

## With a bundler (ESM)

```bash
npm install @formspree/ajax
```

```js
import { initForm } from '@formspree/ajax';
initForm({ formElement: '#my-form', formId: 'mrevrajj' });
```

Data attributes:
- `data-fs-field` — input to receive aria-invalid on error
- `data-fs-error` — displays field-level or form-level error messages
- `data-fs-success` — displays success message after submission
- `data-fs-submit-btn` — disabled during submission, re-enabled on completion

For more information, consult the README at https://github.com/formspree/formspree-js/tree/master/packages/formspree-ajax and the AJAX guide at https://help.formspree.io/hc/en-us/articles/360013470814-Submit-forms-with-JavaScript-AJAX

---

# React

```bash
npm install @formspree/react
```

```jsx
import { useForm, ValidationError } from '@formspree/react';

function ContactForm() {
  const [state, handleSubmit] = useForm('mrevrajj');
  if (state.succeeded) return <p>Thanks!</p>;
  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
      <input type="email" name="email" required />
      <ValidationError field="email" errors={state.errors} />
      <textarea name="message" required />
      <ValidationError field="message" errors={state.errors} />
      <button type="submit" disabled={state.submitting}>Send</button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

For more information, see the @formspree/react documentation at https://github.com/formspree/formspree-js/tree/master/packages/formspree-react and the Formspree React guide at https://help.formspree.io/hc/en-us/articles/360055613373-Formspree-React

---

Based on the tech stack, apply the matching guide above and suggest any needed code changes.

The React guide is the best match for their Vite setup. I am weighing two approaches: updating their existing fetch call with the correct Formspree ID, or refactoring to use the `@formspree/react` `useForm` hook instead.

The existing fetch approach already handles validation and works immediately once the Formspree ID is set. Refactoring to the `@formspree/react` hook would add a new dependency and unnecessary scope. The minimal-change path is preferable.

I decided to refactor Contact.jsx to use the `@formspree/react` `useForm` hook. This aligns with the React guide the user requested. I will preserve their existing UI, validation logic, and styling. I will provide the exact `npm install` command for them to run.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The site uses React and Vite. The React guide is the best match.

The `useForm` hook requires uncontrolled inputs with `name` attributes. This conflicts with the current controlled component pattern. I need to reconcile these two approaches.

Controlled inputs should still work with `useForm` since their values remain in the DOM. I'm considering wrapping `handleSubmit` to run custom validation first, but calling `preventDefault()` might interfere with Formspree's internal submission logic. I need to verify the correct approach in the React documentation.

I found the correct pattern: wrap `handleSubmit` in a custom `onSubmit` handler. Call `e.preventDefault()` first to block submission when fields are incomplete, then invoke `handleSubmit(e)` to let Formspree process the form via its named inputs.

The fetch approach is viable but requires adding a new dependency. Updating the form ID to 'mrevrajj' fixes the immediate bug. Switching to @formspree/react is cleaner but needs npm install.</details></think>

Using the React guide with `@formspree/react` — it fits your stack and keeps the existing form UI and validation.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The success message is hidden by default because its `data-reveal` element starts with `opacity: 0`.

The reveal hook misses the success div because it mounts after submission. The best fix is to remove `data-reveal` from the success div.

Framer-motion or a custom CSS keyframe will animate the success state. The success card will be redesigned to be more prominent and visually appealing.

A "Send another message" button will let users reset the form after success. The contact description may be hidden during the success state. Resetting the Formspree form state is still being checked.

Framer-motion will animate the success panel entrance. The success card will feature a large checkmark circle and a "Message sent" headline. A local `submitted` state may replace Formspree's `state.succeeded` to handle resets.

The success card will include subtext about a 24-hour response window and an optional email fallback. A local `localSuccess` state will drive the success panel, toggled by an effect watching `state.succeeded` so the form can be reset without relying on Formspree's internal state.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The form was disappearing because the success panel had `data-reveal` on it — that class starts at `opacity: 0` and only becomes visible when the scroll reveal hook runs. Since the success message mounts *after* submit, the hook never picks it up, so it stayed invisible.

**What changed:**
- Removed `data-reveal` from the success state
- Added a Framer Motion fade-in so it animates in cleanly after submit
- Replaced the tiny success box with a proper card: accent checkmark, "Message sent" label, headline, and a clear next-steps line with your email as fallback
- Form fades out smoothly before the success card appears

Try submitting again — you should see a clear success card instead of empty space.
